1:
The witnesses agree on the event and divide chiefly over its timing and its cause. The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. Keil and Delitzsch identify Nahash as king of the Ammonites, come to enforce the old claim his people had asserted in the days of Jephthah (Judges 11:13); and they weigh the chronology with care, for the Hebrew fixes no date. From 1 Samuel 12:12, where Israel is said to have desired a king because they saw Nahash coming against them, they conclude that the invasion preceded Saul's election, and note the Septuagint's reading that made the siege begin "about a month" after. Charles Ellicott supplies the frame: Nahash's royal house was in some way bound to David's (2 Samuel 17:25), Jabesh-gilead lay in northern Gilead within Manasseh's territory, and the warlike Ammonite, reckoning the aged Samuel's day a favourable moment, fell on the frontier city.

The Reformed John Gill traces the same Ammonite grudge to Jephthah's time and marks the city's exposure near the border. But it is the Puritan Matthew Henry who presses the moral. The men of Jabesh had "lost the virtue of Israelites, else they would not have thus lost the valour of Israelites"; had they not first broken their covenant with God and forsaken his service, they needed not now to court a covenant with a Gentile nation and offer themselves to serve an Ammonite "without one bold struggle for themselves." Their tame plea, Make a covenant with us, and we will serve thee, is the speech of a people already inwardly beaten. Henry recalls too that this very city had once been destroyed by Israel's own sword of justice for not appearing against the wickedness of Gibeah (Judges 21:10), so that a kind of hard fate seemed to hang over the place.

The Wesleyan John Wesley touches the same chronological knot the others raise: the opening Then means only "about that time," for the event may well have gone before and been the occasion of Israel's demand for a king, though it is possible that Nahash's mere preparation stirred that demand and his actual coming waited until their king was chosen. The one real question dividing the witnesses, then, is narrow — whether Nahash marched before or after Saul was made king — and on the substance they are united: the siege of Jabesh is the God-appointed occasion by which the untried king will be proved, and the abject terms of the besieged expose how far a people that forsakes its covenant with God will stoop to buy peace of its enemies.
2:
Nahash's savage terms — a covenant on condition that every right eye be thrust out, "for a reproach upon all Israel" — draw from the witnesses a single, largely united reading: the cruelty is at once a badge of slavery, a calculated military crippling, and a taunt aimed past Jabesh at the whole nation. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the reasoning. Charles Ellicott takes the horror as an insight into "the barbarous customs of this imperfectly civilised age," and explains the tactic precisely: the inhabitants would be blinded in the right eye while the left was already hidden behind the shield the fighting-man held before him, so that they could never again assist an enemy of Ammon in war. Keil and Delitzsch parse the grammar to show that Nahash himself is the one who would gouge the eyes, and read the whole demand as his revenge for the shame Jephthah had once inflicted on the Ammonites — a defeat he means to repay upon all Israel.

The Wesleyan voices add the ancient testimony. Adam Clarke cites Theodoret and Josephus to the same military point: "He who opposes his shield to the enemy with his left hand thereby hides his left eye, and looks at his enemy with his right eye; he therefore who plucks out that right eye makes men useless in war." John Wesley gathers the twofold purpose in a sentence — the mutilation was "partly for a reproach, as it here follows, and partly to disable them" — and notes the grim calculation in leaving one eye, "that they might be fit to serve in any mean and base office." The Reformed John Gill simply catches the tone, hearing the answer given "in a very haughty and scornful manner."

There is no debate here; the schools converge. Together they see in Nahash's condition the naked face of tyranny: not content to conquer, it must also degrade, and it reaches beyond the beleaguered town to insult the honour of all Israel (v.2). The witnesses let the barbarity stand as its own indictment, and prepare the reader to feel, by contrast, both the desperation of the elders' request in v.3 and the deliverance that Saul, moved by the Spirit, will shortly bring. The reproach Nahash designed for Israel becomes, in the providence of the narrative, the very occasion of Israel's rescue and of the reproach recoiling upon Ammon.
3:
The elders of Jabesh beg seven days' respite to seek help, and the witnesses read the request for what it reveals of Israel's condition and of the ties that would save the city. The grammatical-historical commentators do the historical work. Keil and Delitzsch explain why Nahash granted so strange a delay: he was in no position to storm the town at once, and, given the internal dissolution into which Israel had fallen, he had no expectation that any vigorous help would come; that the messengers must go "unto all the coasts of Israel" shows there was as yet no central government, and that neither Nahash nor the Jabeshites had heard of the election just made. Adam Clarke adds that such conditional surrender — "we will yield if not relieved in so many days" — was a common usage of besieged places, readily accepted by besiegers.

It is Charles Ellicott who supplies the verse's richest note, and with it the reason the appeal would not be in vain. The request, he urges, was made in hope of Saul the Benjamite, for between Benjamin and Jabesh-gilead there had long existed "the closest ties of friendship." When Israel gathered as one man to avenge the crime of Gibeah (Judges 21), Jabesh alone declined the summons, out of friendship for the sinning tribe, and was razed for its disobedience; yet afterward, when the tribes repented and feared that Benjamin's name might perish, they gave the spared maidens of Jabesh to the fighting remnant of Benjamin on the rock of Rimmon (Judges 21:14), and so restored the broken tribe. Jabesh had risen again from its ashes, Benjamin had held up its head and now given Israel its first king; "no wonder, then, that the city in the hour of its sore need should send for succour to Gibeah in Benjamin, and to Saul, the Benjamite king."

The Reformed John Gill notes only that it was the magistrates and principal men who spoke. The witnesses do not divide; together they turn a diplomatic delay into a window on providence. The very disunity that left Israel without a head, and the ancient bond of blood and kindness between Benjamin and Jabesh, are the threads God uses: the seven days Nahash grants in his overconfidence become the appointed space in which deliverance is prepared, and "neither the tribe nor the king failed them in their distress."
4:
The messengers reach Gibeah of Saul, and the people, hearing the news, lift up their voices and weep. The witnesses concentrate on why the messengers came here first, and on the meaning of the tears. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the route. Charles Ellicott observes that though the elders had resolved to send to all the coasts of Israel, we hear only of Gibeah, which may be assumed the first city they sought — both from the ancient friendship with Benjamin and because Gibeah was the residence of the newly-elected king. Keil and Delitzsch catch the significance of Saul's ignorance: he learned nothing of the matter until he came in from the field behind his oxen and found the people already weeping, a sign that neither the Jabeshites nor the nation had yet grasped that they had a king to turn to.

The Reformed John Gill presses the tie of blood that made Gibeah the natural refuge. This was "Gibeah of Saul," so named now for the first time in his honour; and the Benjamites of all the tribes had reason to regard the men of Jabesh, "since it was from thence they had four hundred wives" when the tribe was reduced to six hundred men and needed to be raised up again (Judges 21:14). The debt of kindred ran both ways.

On the weeping itself the witnesses are of one mind, and their judgement is gently severe. Adam Clarke notes that the message was addressed not to Saul but to the people, who "saw no hope of deliverance, and expected that their reproach would be laid on all Israel." Ellicott finds their grief exactly what one would expect of Benjamites hearing of the straits of a city they loved so well; "but though they grieved so deeply, they do not seem of themselves to have been able to devise any plan for its relief, until their great fellow-citizen took the matter in hand." Here is the honest portrait the witnesses agree upon: a people who "would sooner lament their brethren's misery than think of helping them, shed their tears for them than shed their blood." Their tears are real, and useless; they weep as men without a leader — and the very next verses will show what one man, filled with the Spirit of God, can do where a whole city could only mourn.
5:
Saul comes home behind his oxen and asks why the people weep, and the witnesses find in the picture a lesson in the humility of the king-in-waiting. The Puritan Matthew Henry draws it out most fully, marking three graces at once. First, humility: though anointed and accepted, Saul "did not think it below him to know the state of his own flocks," an evidence that he was "not puffed up with his advancement, as those are most apt to be that are raised from a mean estate"; having as yet no revenue settled on him, he would, like Paul, work with his hands rather than be burdensome to the people, remembering Solomon's counsel to look well to one's herds, "for the crown doth not endure to every generation" (Proverbs 27:24). Second, his concern for his neighbours — "Good magistrates are in pain if their subjects are in tears." Third, his zeal for Israel's honour, kindling in the next verse.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same scene through the newness of the monarchy. The regal office was "something quite new in republican Israel," its duties vague and little understood; and given the jealousies already shown when the sons of Belial despised him (1 Samuel 10:27), Saul and his advisers thought it wise to keep the royalty in the background until an occasion should bring it forward. So it is "quite to be understood" that the newly-elected king should still be found at his old pursuits. Ellicott sets him among the heroes summoned from the plough: Gideon called from the threshing-floor, and, from Roman story, Cincinnatus fetched from his furrow to be dictator, and returning to the plough when his country was saved.

The Wesleyan voices concur. John Wesley explains that, being "only anointed king, and not publickly inaugurated," and having had no opportunity to do anything worthy of his place, Saul "thought fit to forbear all royal state, and to retire to his former private life," a life anciently held in great esteem; and he echoes Henry's maxim that "good magistrates are in pain, if their subjects are in tears." Adam Clarke observes that in those ancient times agriculture was well-nigh the only employment, so that generals and rulers were naturally chosen from among husbandmen, quoting Ovid that the consul gave laws to the people "having now laid aside his plough." The Reformed John Gill agrees that Saul had returned to his father's house and its rustic labours. No voice dissents: the man God has chosen to save Israel is discovered doing his daily duty, and his readiness to weep with those who weep, before ever he arms to fight, commends him more than any crown.
6:
The Spirit of God comes upon Saul, and his anger is kindled greatly. The witnesses read the moment as the true beginning of his kingship, and they agree that the anger is a holy one, the gift of the Spirit and not the heat of temper. The Reformed John Gill unfolds what the Spirit wrought: he filled Saul "with pity and compassion to the inhabitants of Jabesh, and with wisdom and prudence" to contrive their relief, "and with fortitude, courage, and resolution" to attempt their deliverance — so the Targum, "the spirit of fortitude from the Lord dwelt on Saul." The anger, Gill adds, was kindled "against Nahash the Ammonite for insulting Israel," and against his threatened cruelty.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the event in the line of the judges. Charles Ellicott notes that nothing could have moved Saul so deeply as this news — touching both the disgrace to Israel over which he had lately been anointed and the peril of his tribe's ancient ally; on a heart thus prepared "the Holy Spirit fell, and endued him with extraordinary wisdom, valour, and power for the great and difficult work which lay before him." He recalls the Spirit coming upon Othniel and the other great deliverers (Judges 3:10), whose immediate result was to impart "new and unusual power to their spirit," enabling them to surmount every danger. Keil and Delitzsch agree that the Spirit of Jehovah came upon him, and that his wrath was kindled precisely "at the shame which the Ammonites had resolved to bring upon all Israel."

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke reads the anger practically, softening any thought of mere rage: it means "no more than that his courage was greatly excited; he felt himself strong for fight, and confident of success." Here the witnesses converge without dispute. The kindled anger is not Saul's private passion but a divinely given indignation at wrong, the same energy that raised up the judges now falling on Israel's first king; and it issues at once, not in a private quarrel, but in wisdom, courage, and a resolve to save. The verse marks the point at which the man who could only be found behind his oxen becomes, by the Spirit, a deliverer — a reminder that the offices God assigns he also equips men to fill.
7:
Saul hews a yoke of oxen in pieces and sends them through all Israel, threatening the like to the oxen of any who will not follow; and the fear of the Lord falls on the people, so that they come out as one man. The witnesses attend to the symbol, to the naming of Samuel, and to the nature of the fear that moved the nation. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the parallel. Keil and Delitzsch compare the act to the Levite's cutting up of his concubine and sending the pieces through Israel (Judges 19:29), which "made a deep impression"; they mark that Saul joins Samuel's name to his own — "a proof that Saul even as king still recognised the authority which Samuel possessed as the prophet of Jehovah" — and insist that "the fear of Jehovah" is no mere panic terror but a true and God-sent awe. Charles Ellicott gathers the analogies of the war-summons from other nations — the Norse war-arrow, the Scottish fire-brand "with both ends dipped in blood" — thinks Saul likely cut the oxen into eleven pieces, one for each tribe, and sees in the people's rising the same Spirit of the Lord that had fallen on Saul now laying "hold of all the people, lifting them up, and giving them new strength and resistless courage, and the mighty feeling that God was with them"; he hears behind it the psalmist's image of the Lord awaking "as one out of sleep" (Psalms 78:65).

The Wesleyan John Wesley reads the pieces shrewdly: Saul sent them "wisely considering that the sight of men's eyes does much more affect their hearts than what they only hear with their ears"; he joins Samuel's name "that hereby he might gain the more authority"; and the fear is "a fear sent upon them by God, that they should not dare to deny their help." From this Wesley draws the abiding lesson that "the fear of God will make men good subjects, good soldiers, and good friends to their country," for "they that fear God will make conscience of their duty to all men, particularly to their rulers." The Reformed John Gill notes only that the oxen were Saul's own or his father's, "which he had just followed out of the field."

The witnesses do not divide. Together they see the Spirit's fire, kindled in one man in v.6, now spreading through the whole people: a rude symbol, a shared dread of God, and a nation that a moment ago could only weep now marching out with one heart. The pairing of Saul's name with Samuel's quietly teaches that the new king does not despise the prophet, and that a rule which honours the word of God will command a willing obedience.
8:
Saul musters the people at Bezek — three hundred thousand of Israel and thirty thousand of Judah — and the witnesses divide over the size of the numbers and, more interestingly, over the separate reckoning of Judah. The grammatical-historical commentators locate the place and defend the figures. Keil and Delitzsch set Bezek some seven hours north of Nablus toward Beisan, and argue that the totals "will not appear too large" once we remember this was not a standing army but a general levy of all the people; in the very distinction drawn between "the children of Judah and the children of Israel," they discern "a trace of that separation of Judah from the rest of the tribes" which would one day harden into schism. Charles Ellicott presses the same point without conceding a late editor's hand: Judah, he says, "always stood apart until there was a king who belonged to itself," and only in David's time first took an active interest in the national welfare; the chronicler notes with "pardonable exultation" the great muster, yet adds "with perhaps an undertone of sadness" that populous Judah furnished but thirty thousand.

The Wesleyan John Wesley reads Judah's separate mention more warmly, and to its honour: it shows "how readily they, to whom the kingdom was promised" (Genesis 49:10), "submitted to their king, though of another tribe," and how willingly they hazarded themselves for their brethren, though they might have pleaded the need of defending their own borders against the Philistines. On the raw numbers Adam Clarke is frankly sceptical: the Septuagint makes it six hundred thousand of Israel and seventy thousand of Judah, and Josephus higher still, so that "these numbers are not all right," and Clarke suspects "even the Hebrew text to be exaggerated by the mistake or design of some ancient scribe." The Reformed John Gill notes Bezek as the meeting-place and records the old alternate reading that took the word not as a place but as "a stone" or "fragment" used in the counting.

The differences are real but bounded. The witnesses agree that the muster was vast, that Judah is set apart deliberately, and that the numbers, whatever their exact value, testify to the sudden unity the Spirit had wrought. Whether one hears in Judah's separate line a note of coming division (Keil, Ellicott) or of present loyalty to a king not its own (Wesley), all read the scene as the first great gathering of a people that a week before had no head — a nation come out "as one man" to redeem the honour of Israel.
9:
Saul and Samuel send the messengers back with a promise: "To morrow, by that time the sun be hot, ye shall have help"; and the men of Jabesh, hearing it, are glad. The source here is thin, and the witnesses, chiefly the grammatical-historical commentators, draw out its few plain points with care. Keil and Delitzsch explain the timing: the deliverance is pledged "when the sun shines hot," that is, about noon — so that relief would arrive well within the seven days Nahash had granted, and indeed before he could suppose the term expired. They note too the human touch, that the messengers "had been waiting to see the result of Saul's appeal," and were now despatched with news that turned their errand of despair into one of hope. Charles Ellicott adds the geographical measure that makes the promise credible: "the distance from Bezek to Jabesh was not much over twenty miles," a march an aroused army could accomplish in a night, so that the pledge of help by noon was no idle boast.

The Reformed John Gill is content to fix the speakers, taking the plural "they said" of Saul and Samuel together — the king and the prophet answering the men of Jabesh with one voice, as they had summoned Israel with one name in v.7.

Slight as the verse is, the witnesses let its own words and setting preach. The gladness of Jabesh is the answer of a people who "had wept themselves dry," as Matthew Henry says of them elsewhere, and now hear that rescue is at hand at the appointed hour. There is a quiet fidelity in the scene: Saul, moved by the Spirit and confident of success, does not merely gather an army but sends word ahead to steady the fainting, keeping faith with the besieged before ever he meets the enemy. The promise is exact — a day, an hour — and the narrative will show it kept even better than its terms, for the relief will come not by noon but in the morning watch (v.11). The gladness of Jabesh at a bare word of help commends the assurance that a God-appointed deliverer inspires, and prepares the reader to see the word fulfilled.
10:
The men of Jabesh send word to Nahash, "To morrow we will come out unto you, and ye shall do with us all that seemeth good unto you"; and the witnesses read the message as a deliberate ruse of war, weighing its truthfulness even as they admire its craft. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch state it plainly: the announcement was "an untruth by which they hoped to assure the besiegers, so that they might be fallen upon unexpectedly by the advancing army of Saul, and thoroughly beaten." The words are chosen to sound like surrender — the very terms of abject submission from v.3 — so that Nahash, lulled into security, should relax his watch and be caught between the sally of the city and the onset of Saul.

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke reads the stratagem the same way and points its irony: the Jabeshites "concealed the information they had received of Saul's promised assistance." They did indeed come out to the Ammonites on the morrow, "but it was in a different manner to what the Ammonites expected" — not as captives yielding their eyes, but as men falling on a foe already surrounded. The Reformed John Gill simply notes that the message was addressed "to Nahash the Ammonite," binding the verse to the deception it records.

The witnesses do not moralise heavily here, and their restraint is instructive. They see the ambiguous speech of the besieged not as treachery but as the permitted craft of the weak against a cruel oppressor: the men of Jabesh owed no obligation to warn the enemy of the help assured them, and their words, though calculated to mislead, kept the letter of a promise they meant to fulfil in an unlooked-for sense. Behind the human cunning the narrative keeps the divine hand in view — Nahash, so secure in his advantage, is being drawn by his own overconfidence into the trap that his seven-day boast had set for him. The verse thus quietly advances the theme of the chapter: the reproach the Ammonite designed for Israel is folding back upon his own head, and the deliverance promised by Saul is about to be sprung at the very hour of the enemy's ease.
11:
At dawn Saul divides the people into three companies, breaks into the camp in the morning watch, and smites the Ammonites till the heat of the day, until the survivors are so scattered that "two of them were not left together." The witnesses read the victory for its tactics, its completeness, and its providential timing. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the military detail. Keil and Delitzsch note that the three divisions forced their way into the camp "from three different sides" in the morning watch, between three and six o'clock, and routed the foe so utterly that no two were left together — comparing the threefold arrangement to Gideon's against Midian (Judges 7:16). Adam Clarke reconstructs the march: Saul "probably began his march in the evening, passed Jordan in the night, and reached the camp of the Ammonites by daybreak," and reads the scattering as proof "that the rout was complete." Charles Ellicott places the assault in the last of the three night-watches, the old Hebrew division, so that the first onslaught fell "some time between two and six a.m.," the battle and pursuit continuing for many hours.

The Puritan Matthew Henry gives the scene its warmth of devotion, admiring both the swiftness and the bravery. Saul was better than his word: he had promised help "by that time the sun was hot," yet brought it "before day, in the morning-watch"; and Henry seizes the psalmist's phrase — "Whom God helps, he helps right early" (Psalms 46:5). While the Ammonites "lay dreaming of the triumphs they expected that day," Saul was in the midst of their host, his three columns surrounding them "on every side, so that they could have neither heart nor time to make head against them." The Reformed John Gill anchors the timing, noting it was "on the morrow" after the messengers returned and the men of Jabesh had lulled the besiegers with their promise.

The witnesses do not divide; they unite in reading the rout as God's own salvation through his newly-Spirit-endued king. The tactics are sound, the courage real, the timing exact — and yet the deeper note they strike is that the victory is the Lord's, "wrought salvation in Israel," as Saul himself will confess in v.13. The man found behind his oxen has, in a single night, delivered a city and vindicated the honour of Israel; and the completeness of the overthrow — not two of the enemy left together — sets the reproach of Nahash wholly upon Ammon's own head.
12:
Flushed with victory, the people turn to Samuel: "Who is he that said, Shall Saul reign over us? bring the men, that we may put them to death." The witnesses read the demand as a mixture of loyalty and blood-lust, and mark to whom it is addressed. The grammatical-historical commentators trace the reference and the appeal. Keil and Delitzsch connect the words to "the exclamation of the worthless people" who had despised Saul at his election (1 Samuel 10:27), and note that the people bring their motion to Samuel because Saul "had so thoroughly acted the part of a king in gaining this victory." Charles Ellicott finds the seer's abiding weight strikingly shown: even "in the first flush of this great victory," it is to Samuel that the people look to bring the offenders to punishment, remembering that the royal summons had gone out in the joint names of Saul and Samuel (v.7).

The Puritan Matthew Henry weighs the people's temper with characteristic shrewdness. They "took this occasion to show their jealousy for the honour of Saul," bringing the motion to Samuel as judge, "for they knew Saul would not be judge in his own cause." Yet Henry sees the baser strain: Saul's success "went further with them to confirm his title than either his choice by lot or Samuel's anointing him"; they "had not courage to move for the prosecution of those that opposed him when he himself looked mean," but now that "his victory made him look great, nothing would serve but they must be put to death." The Wesleyan Adam Clarke is sharper still on the mob's spirit: "flushed with victory and proud of their leader, they wished to give him a proof of their attachment by slaying, even in cool blood, the persons who were at first averse"; and he adds the mordant observation that "the common soldier is scarcely ever inspired by his victory to acts of magnanimity — he has shed blood, he wishes to shed more." The Reformed John Gill pauses only to wonder how the aged Samuel kept pace with so swift a march, suggesting he followed gently and met them soon after the battle.

The witnesses do not divide over the sense; they agree that the people's zeal is real but sits ill with the day's mercy. Their eagerness to kill the "sons of Belial" springs less from justice than from the intoxication of triumph — and the contrast is precisely what makes Saul's restraint in v.13 shine. Where the crowd would crown a victory with vengeance, the king they have just proved will crown it with pardon.
13:
Saul cuts short the cry for blood: "There shall not a man be put to death this day; for to day the Lord hath wrought salvation in Israel." The witnesses are united in admiration, and read the answer as at once magnanimous, politic, and devout. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh both its wisdom and its piety. Charles Ellicott calls it "a wise, as well as a generous, decision," for "anything like a bloody vengeance would have been the commencement of future feuds" between the new king and the powerful families that had opposed him; by this refusal to avenge himself, Saul "taught kings to be how truly a royal virtue was forgiveness of all past wrongs," and showed a heart "full of humble reverent piety." Keil and Delitzsch find in it proof "not only of his magnanimity, but also of his genuine piety," and cite Seb. Schmidt: since Jehovah had that day shown such clemency in overlooking their sins and granting a glorious victory, it was "only right they should follow His example, and forgive their neighbours' sins without bloodshed."

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke sets the king's grace against the soldiers' cruelty: the refusal "was as much to Saul's credit as the lately proposed measure was to the discredit of his soldiers." The Reformed John Gill reads it as "lenity as well as prudence" — it was in his power alone as king to pardon, and politic to do so at his reign's beginning — while soberly adding "that this temper did not always continue with him."

It is the Puritan Matthew Henry who lifts the verse highest. Saul spared the men on two grounds: because it was a day of joy — "since God has been so good to us all, let us not be harsh one to another" — and because he hoped an enemy reclaimed is better than an enemy slain, "for all good princes consider that their power is for edification, not for destruction." Then Henry turns the mercy into a figure of Christ: "The once despised Saviour will at length be acknowledged by all as the Lord's own anointed king. As yet, upon his mercy-seat, he receives the submission of rebels, and even pleads their cause; but shortly, from his righteous tribunal, he will condemn all who persist in opposing him." Here the witnesses land together: the king's clemency mirrors the clemency of God, and points beyond Saul to the greater Anointed who, in the day of his salvation, spares the very rebels who despised his reign.
14:
Samuel calls the people to Gilgal to "renew the kingdom there," and the witnesses read the summons as the seer's wise seizing of the moment to confirm a king the nation had once received grudgingly. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies Gilgal as "the well-known sanctuary of that name," in the Jordan valley near Jericho, "the scene of many of the most striking events in Israelitic history," and dwells on Samuel's generosity: remembering "the many murmurings on the occasion of the original royal election at Mizpeh," where the people had by no means unanimously accepted the Benjamite, "the seer, with striking generosity to one who superseded him in his position as judge, again presented the hero Saul to Israel as their anointed king."

The Wesleyan voices commend the same prudence and fidelity. John Wesley finds "Samuel's great prudence and fidelity to Saul" evident here: "he suspended the confirmation of Saul at first, whilst the generality of the people were disaffected, and now, when he had given such eminent proof of his princely virtues, and when the people's hearts were eagerly set upon him, he takes this as the fittest season for that work"; and Wesley glosses "renew" as "confirm our former choice." Adam Clarke reasons to the same end from the privacy of the first anointing: Saul's original unction "was a very private act, and his being appointed king was not known to the people in general"; now that he "had shown himself worthy to command," Samuel takes advantage of the moment "to gain the general consent in his favour" — and Clarke reports that "Josephus says that Saul was anointed a second time at this convocation." The Reformed John Gill notes that Samuel spoke "agreeing to what Saul had said," and partly to draw the people off from their demand for the offenders' lives while their mood was mild.

The witnesses do not seriously divide, though Clarke's report of a second anointing opens a question the next verse will press. On the substance they agree: the victory over Ammon has done what lot and private unction could not, winning the willing hearts of all Israel, and Samuel — far from clinging to the office Saul had displaced — moves at once to fix the crown firmly on the head God had chosen. The renewal at Gilgal is the prophet's own generous act, turning a moment of triumph into the settled establishment of the kingdom, "that he might not retire again to his obscurity."
15:
All the people go to Gilgal and there "made Saul king before the Lord," offering peace offerings and rejoicing greatly; and the witnesses divide, gently, over one question — whether Saul was anointed afresh — while agreeing on the meaning of the day. The grammatical-historical commentators are cautious about a second unction. Charles Ellicott warns that "we must not understand with the Septuagint that Saul was anointed afresh at Gilgal"; the Greek adds "and Samuel anointed Saul king there," but the convention "was nothing more than a solemn national confirmation of the popular election at Mizpeh." He takes "before the Lord" to imply "the presence of the Ark, or of the high priest with the mystic Urim and Thummim," and cites Bishop Wordsworth that "they made Saul king" means the people would no longer let him lead a private life, but "made him assume the royal state and authority to which he had been appointed by God." Keil and Delitzsch likewise resist the idea of a fresh anointing: David's later unctions prove the rite "could be repeated" under certain circumstances, but not that it was repeated here, "for had the ceremony been performed, it would no doubt have been mentioned."

Against this the Wesleyan Adam Clarke holds that a second anointing is "likely from these words": Saul "was now publicly acknowledged, and there was no gainsayer." Clarke also preserves a learned correspondent's long note comparing Saul's war-summons to later customs — the Highland fiery cross and the Scandinavian death-token recorded by Olaus Magnus — and finding in the right-eye mutilation of v.2 "a military reason, corroborative of the truth of history." Yet even Clarke sounds the coming shadow: "Thus far Saul acted well, and the kingdom seemed to be confirmed in his hand; but soon, through imprudence, he lost it." The Reformed John Gill notes simply that all the people agreed to the motion and marched to Gilgal, and the Wesleyan John Wesley glosses "made" as "they owned and accepted him for their king."

The debate over the anointing is narrow; on the substance the witnesses are one. Gilgal sets the seal, "before the Lord," on the kingship God had chosen and the victory had vindicated: a people once divided now rejoices with sacrifice, and the crown rests, for this bright hour, securely on Saul. Yet the witnesses close, with Clarke, on a sober minor key — the king who "thus far acted well" will soon, by his own folly, forfeit what this day so gladly bestowed, so that the joy of Gilgal stands as both a genuine height and a warning of how a well-begun reign may be undone.
